title: The role of fascia in holding emotional memory
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The body keeps more than we know

I spent years thinking trauma lived in my head. In the stories I told myself. In the flashbacks and nightmares and the way my mind would go blank at the wrong moment. I read books about cognitive behavioral therapy. I learned to reframe my thoughts. I practiced gratitude and positive affirmations until my jaw ached from the effort of pretending.

But my body never got the memo.

My shoulders stayed hunched. My lower back locked up every time someone raised their voice. My hips felt like concrete blocks filled with something I couldn't name. And no amount of thinking my way through it changed a thing.

Here's the thing. The body keeps the score in ways we're only beginning to understand. And one of the most overlooked players in this whole mess is something you've probably never thought about: your fascia.

Fascia is this web of connective tissue that wraps around every muscle, organ, nerve, and bone in your body. It's like a three-dimensional spiderweb that holds everything together. And it's not just structural. It's communicative. It's responsive. And according to researchers like Dr. Robert Schleip, who directs the fascia research project at Ulm University in Germany, it's packed with sensory nerve endings that make it one of our richest sensory organs.

Think about that for a second. The stuff that holds your muscles in place is also listening. It's sensing tension, pressure, and stretch. And it remembers.

Stay with me here. This is where it gets wild.

When you experience something traumatic, your nervous system goes into protection mode. Your muscles contract. Your breath shortens. Your body braces for impact. And if that trauma doesn't get resolved, if you don't get to complete the survival response, those contractions don't fully let go. They become chronic. They become your new normal.

Something happens in the body when safety has been absent for a long time. The muscles forget how to relax. Not metaphorically - literally. The fascia tightens. The diaphragm restricts. The psoas - that deep hip flexor that connects your spine to your legs - stays chronically contracted, ready to run or curl into a ball at any moment.

Bessel van der Kolk's research on yoga and trauma showed something worth noticing. Traumatized people who practiced yoga showed changes in their brain scans - specifically in the insula, the part of the brain responsible for interoception, for feeling what's happening inside your own body. Many trauma survivors have learned to disconnect from body sensation entirely. It was too dangerous to feel. Yoga - gentle, trauma-informed yoga - helped them come back.

I'm not saying yoga fixes everything. I'm saying the body needs to be included in healing. You can't think your way out of something that lives below thought. You have to feel your way through it. Slowly. With support. At your own pace. There's no rushing this.

And your fascia, being the responsive web that it is, starts to thicken and tighten around those patterns. It literally changes shape to hold the memory of that trauma in your tissues.

I remember the first time a bodyworker touched my psoas muscle. That deep hip flexor that connects your spine to your legs. I wasn't prepared for what happened. As her hands found the tension there, I started shaking. Not from cold. Not from fear. From something ancient moving through me. Tears came without warning. Images from a childhood I thought I'd dealt with surfaced like they'd been waiting for permission.

They had been waiting. In my fascia.

Dr. John Upledger, an osteopathic physician who developed craniosacral therapy, wrote extensively about how the body stores "energy cysts" in the connective tissues. These are pockets of unresolved tension that hold the imprint of past experiences. He believed that the body's tissues carry a kind of cellular memory, and that releasing that tension could bring up the emotional content that was stored with it.

I've felt this in my own body more times than I can count. A release in my chest that brings up grief I didn't know I was carrying. A softening in my jaw that unlocks anger I'd been clenching against for decades. It's not mystical. It's not pseudoscience. It's your connective tissue responding to the fact that it's been holding a load it was never meant to carry.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link). Peter Levine's work on how animals release trauma through physical completion has been foundational for understanding why the body needs to move through these states, not just talk about them.

How fascia holds tension and why it matters

Let me be specific about how this works. Fascia is made up of collagen and elastin fibers arranged in a wavy pattern. When you're relaxed, those fibers are loose and flexible. But when you're under stress, your nervous system signals your muscles to contract, and your fascia responds by pulling those fibers tighter. It becomes less fluid. More rigid. Almost like it's bracing for a blow that might come at any moment.

Now imagine that bracing lasts for years. Decades. Your fascia adapts to that chronic tension by laying down more collagen fibers in the direction of the pull. It's like your body is building a cast around the trauma. It's trying to protect you. But that protection comes at a cost.

Your range of motion decreases. Your posture changes. You start moving through the world like you're wearing armor you can't take off. And because fascia is all connected, tension in one area pulls on everything else. Tight hips can lead to low back pain. Tight shoulders can create tension headaches. A tight jaw can affect your vision, your breathing, even your balance.

Right? You've probably experienced this. You notice your shoulders are up around your ears and you can't seem to drop them. Or your lower back aches after a stressful day even though you didn't do anything physically demanding. That's your fascia holding the emotional weight you've been carrying.

Dr. Thomas Myers, author of "Anatomy Trains," mapped out how these fascial lines connect different parts of the body. He showed that a restriction in your foot can affect your neck. That emotional tension held in your gut can show up as tension in your throat. It's all connected. It's all communicating.

I've worked with a lot of trauma survivors who told me they felt "stuck" in their bodies. Like they were living in a prison made of their own flesh. And I get that. I really do. Because when your fascia is holding patterns of protection that haven't been updated in years, every movement feels effortful. Every breath feels shallow. You're not living in your body. You're just occupying it.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). Deb Dana's work on polyvagal theory offers concrete ways to help your nervous system find safety, which in turn allows your fascia to begin releasing those held patterns.

Let me ask you something. When was the last time you felt truly relaxed in your body? Not distracted. Not numbed out. Actually relaxed. If you can't remember, that tells you something important. Your nervous system may have forgotten what rest feels like. It may have decided, somewhere along the way, that rest is dangerous.

Deb Dana talks about this - how the dorsal vagal state can look like relaxation from the outside but actually feels like collapse on the inside. There's a difference between choosing to rest and being unable to move. There's a difference between peace and shutdown. Your body knows the difference, even if the people around you can't tell.

Notice your hands right now. Are they clenched? Open? Cold? Warm? Your hands tell a story about your autonomic state. Cold hands often mean sympathetic activation - blood has moved to the core, preparing for threat. Warm, relaxed hands suggest ventral vagal engagement - safety. It's a small thing to notice. But small things are where healing lives.

What release actually feels like

Here's what I want you to know. Releasing fascia is not like stretching a tight muscle. It's not like getting a massage that feels good in the moment. Real fascial release can be intense. It can bring up emotions you didn't know were there. It can make you feel raw and exposed and weirdly vulnerable.

And that's okay. That's actually the point.

When you start working with your fascia, whether through bodywork, gentle movement, or specific practices like myofascial release, you're asking your body to let go of patterns it's been holding for a long time. Those patterns kept you safe. They helped you survive. Your body doesn't know that the threat is over. It just knows that letting go feels dangerous.

So you might shake. You might cry. You might feel heat or cold or tingling in parts of your body that have been numb for years. You might have memories surface that don't make logical sense. You might feel rage or terror or deep, bone-level grief.

Let it happen. Don't try to control it. Don't try to make it mean something. Just let your body move through what it needs to move through.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Bessel van der Kolk's research makes clear that trauma is stored in the body, and that healing requires working with the body directly. Not just talking about what happened, but helping the body release what it's been holding.

Starting where you are

You don't need to book an expensive session with a specialist to start working with your fascia. You can begin right now, wherever you are. Here's one thing to try.

Take a breath. Just one. And as you exhale, let your jaw go slack. Let your tongue rest at the bottom of your mouth. Let your eyes soften. Let your shoulders drop. Let your belly release. Don't force anything. Just invite your body to let go of what it's been holding.

Notice what happens. Maybe nothing. Maybe a small shift. Maybe a wave of something you can't name. That's your fascia responding to the possibility of release. That's your nervous system getting the message that maybe, just maybe, it's safe enough to let some of that armor go.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). Peter Levine's deeper exploration of how the body processes and releases trauma offers a framework for understanding these experiences when they arise.

I won't pretend this is easy. It's not. Releasing fascia that's been holding trauma for years can be uncomfortable. It can stir up things you've worked hard to bury. And there's no guarantee that it will all come out at once or that you'll feel better immediately.

But here's what I know from my own body and from the bodies of the people I've worked with. When you start to release those fascial patterns, something shifts. Your breath gets deeper. Your movement gets easier. You start to feel like you're actually living in your body instead of just surviving inside it. And that, honestly, is worth every uncomfortable moment along the way.

Your fascia has been holding your story. Not in words, but in tension. Not in memories, but in patterns of protection. And when you start to unwind those patterns, you're not just releasing physical tension. You're giving yourself permission to let go of the past in the most literal way possible.

Your body wants to heal. It's been waiting for you to listen. And now you know where to start.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - For those exploring somatic approaches
  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful
  • In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) - Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing
